coredata.dc:title: Investigation of silicon doped carbon dots/Carboxymethyl cellulose gel platform with tunable afterglow and dynamic multistage anticounterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Zhengdong
Shen, Yuan
Liu, Yang
Wang, Jiaqi
Ma, Mingjian
Pan, Jiangbo
Wang, Di
Wang, Chengyu
Li, Jian
coredata.dc:description: The remarkable optical properties of carbon dots, particularly their tunable room-temperature phosphorescence, have garnered significant interest. However, challenges such as aggregation propensity and complex phosphorescence control via energy level manipulation during synthesis persist. Addressing these issues, we present a facile gel platform for tunable afterglow materials. This involves chemically cross-linking biomass-derived silicon-doped carbon dots with carboxymethylcellulose and incorporating non-precious metal salts (BaCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, ZnBr2, ZnSO4) to enhance phosphorescence. Metal salts boost intersystem crossing via spin–orbit coupling, elevating triplet state transitions and activating phosphorescence. Chemical bonding and salt-induced coordination/electrostatic interactions establish confinement effects, suppressing non-radiative transitions. Diverse salt-gel interactions yield gels with tunable phosphorescence lifetimes (9.48 ms to 32.13–492.39 ms), corresponding to afterglow durations ranging from 3.20 to 11.86 s. With its broad tunability and high recognition, this gel material exhibits promising potential for dynamic multilevel anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si-doped carbon dots
Carboxymethyl cellulose gel
Room temperature phosphorescence
Tunable afterglow
Dynamic multistage anticounterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the DCG and DCG/Salt
DCG/Salt structural and enhanced phosphorescence mechanism analysis

The authors also would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR of CMCS, Si-CDs and APTES. XPS spectra of Si-CDs. (b) Full spectrum, (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s, (f) Si 2p. (g) TEM and (h) HRTEM image of Si-CDs. (i) Size distribution histogram of Si-CDs.

(a) FTIR of CMC and DCG. XPS spectra of DCG. (b) Full spectrum, (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s, (f) Si 2p. (g) UV–Visible absorption spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/Salt. SEM images of (h) DCG and (i) DCG/ZnSO4.

(a) Statistical diagram of the afterglow duration of DCG/Salt at each concentration. (b) Afterglow photos of DCG/Salt, CMC and DCG at 0.4 M concentration. (c) Fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/Salt. (d) Phosphor emission spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/Salt. (e) Phosphor excitation spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/ZnSO4. (f) Decay diagram of phosphor lifetime after fitting for Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/Salt. (g) Column diagram of phosphor lifetime for Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/Salt. (h) Relationship between ion radius, phosphor lifetime and afterglow duration.
Binding Energy of each chemical bond in XPS spectra of DCG/Salt. (a) CO, (b) CN, (C) CO, (d) NC, (e) NH–CO, (f) NH, (g) OC, (h) OC, (i) OH.
(a) Structure of DCG/Salt. (b) Jablonski diagram representing the luminescence mechanism of DCG/Salt, Abs.: Absorption, Fluo.: Fluorescence, Vib.: Vibration, Phos.: Phosphorescence.

Schematic of dynamic multistage security application. (a) Schematic of the materials used in the pattern and their positions. (b) Photographs of the digital security pattern under daylight. (c) Photographs of the digital security pattern under 365 nm UV light. (d) Photographs of the digital security pattern at different times intercepted after the 365 nm UV light is turned off. (e) Photographs of the flower security pattern at different times intercepted after the 365 nm UV light is turned off Photographs.

head.ce:title: Investigation of silicon doped carbon dots/Carboxymethyl cellulose gel platform with tunable afterglow and dynamic multistage anticounterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology, Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The remarkable optical properties of carbon dots, particularly their tunable room-temperature phosphorescence, have garnered significant interest. However, challenges such as aggregation propensity and complex phosphorescence control via energy level manipulation during synthesis persist. Addressing these issues, we present a facile gel platform for tunable afterglow materials. This involves chemically cross-linking biomass-derived silicon-doped carbon dots with carboxymethylcellulose and incorporating non-precious metal salts (BaCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, ZnBr2, ZnSO4) to enhance phosphorescence. Metal salts boost intersystem crossing via spin–orbit coupling, elevating triplet state transitions and activating phosphorescence. Chemical bonding and salt-induced coordination/electrostatic interactions establish confinement effects, suppressing non-radiative transitions. Diverse salt-gel interactions yield gels with tunable phosphorescence lifetimes (9.48 ms to 32.13–492.39 ms), corresponding to afterglow durations ranging from 3.20 to 11.86 s. With its broad tunability and high recognition, this gel material exhibits promising potential for dynamic multilevel anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting technology is an important means to solve the problem of counterfeit products damaging people's interests. Based on this technology, many anti-counterfeiting materials have been developed, including watermark materials [1], laser holographic materials [2] and photoluminescent materials [3,4] and so on. As an emerging anti-counterfeiting material, photoluminescent materials have received extensive attention due to their luminous mode and stability, including fluorescent materials and room temperature phosphorescent materials [5]. Fluorescent materials have been widely used in the field of anti-counterfeiting [6,7], but their shortcomings such as static output information, easy to be interfered by excited light and easy to be copied limit their further development [8]. Room-temperature phosphorescent materials play an important role in the anti-counterfeiting field due to their anti-excitation light interference and long emission lifetime, which are usually considered as ideal anti-counterfeiting materials [9]. Up to now, many room temperature phosphorescent materials have been developed, mainly including rare earth based materials [10], precious metal complexes [11], and pure organic compounds [12]. Due to its disadvantages such as high cost, high toxicity, complex preparation method and poor processability, people have been trying to explore new room temperature phosphorescent materials. In addition, on account of the complexity of modulating phosphorescence by controlling the energy level structure of the material during preparation, achieving sustainably tunable phosphorescence emission remains a great challenge.

In recent years, anti-counterfeiting materials related to natural polymer gels have been widely reported for their good forming and integration properties [13–15]. As an excellent backbone material of gel materials, cellulose is not only the most abundant natural polymer, but also it and its derivatives have good mechanical properties. However, their photoluminescence properties are poor and usually require the introduction of additional emission sources [16–18]. Since cellulose and its derivatives have a large number of active functional groups on their chains, which can provide sites for binding with emission sources, they can be used as good carriers of luminous materials [19]. Among many emission sources, carbon dots, as a new type of luminous materials, have the advantages of strong photoluminescence performance and stability, and have been widely used in the field of anti-counterfeiting [20]. As a natural polysaccharide derivative, carboxymethyl chitosan has been widely selected for the synthesis of carbon dots due to its wide range of sources and renewable properties. However, polysaccharide based carbon dots usually have poor luminous properties, so polysaccharide based carbon dots with strong luminous properties can be prepared by doping silicon to adjust the band structure of carbon dots [21].

At present, the application of carbon dots in the field of phosphorescent materials still faces two major challenges. Firstly, the phosphorescence of carbon dots is due to the decay of triplet energy levels, so the traditional fluorescent carbon dots usually do not have long-lived phosphorescence performance. Generally, the transition from excited singlet to excited triplet of carbon dot is prohibited, so activating phosphorescence at carbon dots at room temperature is very challenging. Secondly, due to the small size (usually between 2–10 nm) [22] and the rich surface functional groups (such as carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, etc.) of the carbon dots, they are easy to agglomerate and difficult to process. Based on the concept of sustainable development, it is a reasonable choice to combine natural renewable cellulose as a carrier and carbon dots to complement each other's shortcomings.

In this article, we propose a generalized gel platform for the preparation of tunable persistence materials with blue fluorescence and blue-green phosphorescence by adsorbing different non-precious metal salts. The gel is formed by choosing doped silicon in carbon dots as crosslinker and luminophores, connecting to carboxymethyl cellulose matrix by bonding, while forming first-order limiting structures. The carbon dots/carboxymethylcellulose gels are complexed with metal ions through rich functional groups to form secondary limiting structures. This double confinement structure limits the non-radiative transition caused by the vibration of the carbon dots luminophores and increases the phosphor lifetime of the material. Moreover, the metal salt enhances the intersystem crossover (ISC) by spin–orbit coupling (SOC) with the gel, increasing the chance of triplet transition. Therefore, the selection of metal salts adsorbed on the gel platform to achieve the modulation of the afterglow duration provides an effective solution for the production of tunable sustained luminescent materials.

Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS, ≥80 % degree of substitution), Sodium carboxymethylcellulose (CMC, MW = 250000, DS = 1.2, 400–800 mPa.s), (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane(APTES, purity98.0 %), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, purity98.0 %) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co. N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, purity98.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Zinc sulfate (ZnSO4), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), zinc bromide (ZnBr2) were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co. Magnesium chloride (MgCl2) were purchased from Tianjin Ruijinte Chemical Co. Calcium chloride (CaCl2) were purchased from Tianjin Reference Chemical Reagent Co. Barium chloride (BaCl2) were purchased from Tianjin Dongli district Tianda chemical reagent factory. Manganese chloride (MnCl2) were purchased from Tianjin Xinbote Chemical Co. Ferric chloride (FeCl3) were purchased from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Preparation Co. Copper chloride (CuCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), aluminum chloride (AlCl3) and chromic chloride (CrCl3) were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. Absolute ethyl alcohol were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co. These chemicals are analytically pure and have not been further purified prior to use These chemicals are analytically pure and have not been further purified prior to use. Deionized water produced by Clever-Q30 UT was used in this study.

0.5 g of CMCS powder was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, and APTES (5:1 M ratio to CMCS structural units) was added and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. The reaction was transferred to a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and then placed in a blower oven at a constant temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. After cooled to room temperature, the reaction was filtered using a 0.22 μm micropore membrane. filtered, and then dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 3 days. The dialysate was concentrated by rotary evaporator and freeze-dried to obtain yellow powder Si-CDs with blue fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the DCG and DCG/Salt
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolve 0.1 g of CMC powder in 7 mL of deionized water and add 1 mL of 50 mg/mL aqueous solution of Si-CDs. Stirred for 10 min and then sonicated for 10 min to make Si-CDs uniformly dispersed between the CMC chains. Add 1 mL of freshly prepared 0.5 mM EDC aqueous solution under stirring, and then add 1 mL of freshly prepared 0.5 mM NHS aqueous solution after stirring for another 5 min. After 24 h of reaction at room temperature, the unreacted material was removed by dialysis in 50 % ethanol aqueous solution, and the faint yellow transparent gel DCG with blue fluorescence emission was obtained.10 mL of 0.4 M salt solution was added to the DCG, and the excess salt on the surface was washed off with deionized water after 48 h of natural adsorption at room temperature, and the material DCG/Salt was obtained by lyophilization.

The samples were excited using a 365nmLED UV flashlight (Ningbo Yunfeng Stationery Co.) and photographed using a smartphone (Redmi K30 Ultra Xiaomi China) to record the sample luminescence. The shutter time was fixed at 1/30 in professional mode with a sensitivity of ISO 3200, a resolution of 1080p, and a frame rate of 30. Matlab was then used to identify the grayscale of the image, which was used to predict and identify the fluorescence lifetime and afterglow duration of the sample. Consecutive frames with a gray scale greater than 10 were recorded after the UV light was turned off and the afterglow duration was calculated.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained using the PerkinElmer Frontier spectrometer produced by PerkinElmer, USA, with a measurement range of 4000–550 and a resolution of 2 cm-1. The UV–visible diffuse reflection spectra was measured using the UV–visible spectrophotometer TU-1950 produced by PERSEE, China. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using K-alpha, an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer produced by Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with the transmission electron microscope JEM-2100 produced by the Electron Optics Laboratory, Japan. The phosphorescence emission spectra and the phosphorescence lifetime decay maps were obtained by testing with the fluorescence spectrometer FLS1000 produced by Edinburgh, UK. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with LS-55, a fluorescence spectrophotometer produced by PerkinElmer, USA. Thermogravimetric analysis TGAs is carried out using the STA 6000-SQ8 analyzer produced by PerkinElmer in the USA, heated from 40 °C to 800 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere at a rate of 10 °C/min. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by using an X-ray diffractometer X'Pert3 Powder produced by PANalytical, Netherlands.

FTIR spectra were used to detect the occurrence of the reaction and the FTIR spectra of CMCS, APTES and Si-CDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The pure carboxymethyl chitosan powder shows stretching vibrational absorption bands of hydroxyl and amino groups at 3410 cm−1. The peaks near 2900 cm−1 and 2870 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CH. The peak at 1052 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of CH of the pyranose ring. the peaks at 1412 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric –COO– stretching vibration peaks and symmetric vibrational stretching peaks, which are associated with the presence of –COONa [23]. The peak at 1310 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric vibrational peak of CN [24]. Comparing the infrared absorption curves of CMCS, new peaks appeared in the curves of Si-CDs, the asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of Si–O–Si at 1002 cm−1 and 690 cm−1, the peak of SiOC at 1188 cm−1, and the bending vibration peak of Si–OH at 923 cm−1. 1002 cm−1 and 690 cm−1 are the asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of Si–O–Si, which proved that Si–OH produced by APTES hydrolysis was dehydrated and condensed to form siloxane structure again. The peaks at 1002 cm−1 and 690 cm−1 are the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of Si–O–Si, which proves that the Si–OH produced by the hydrolysis of APTES was dehydrated and condensed to form the siloxane structure [25]. The disappearance of the –CH3 peak at 2974 cm−1 present in APTES proved that the hydrolysis of APTES was complete [26]. The peaks related to –COO– were retained in Si-CDs, indicating that Si-CDs have –COOH and –COONa. more pronounced asymmetric and symmetric telescopic vibrational absorption peaks of the CH bond in –CH2– and –CH3 were produced at 2929 cm−1 and 2864 cm−1 due to the addition of APTES. 1310 cm−1 asymmetric CN vibrational peaks still existed and more obvious bending vibrational peaks of –NH2 were produced at 3352 cm−1 and 3284 cm−1, which proved that the amino group of APTES was retained on the surface of Si-CDs after the reaction [26].

In order to further investigate the molecular structure and functional group composition of Si-CDs, XPS tests were performed and the high-resolution XPS spectra were processed by peak fitting. The full XPS spectra in Fig. 1(b) shows that the Si-CDs contain the elements of C, N, O, Si, and Na. In C1s spectra in Fig. 1(c), C–Si, CC, CN, CO/CO correspond to 283.91, 284.80, 285.93, 288.26 eV [27,28]. In the N1s spectra in Fig. 1(d), N–Si, NC, NH correspond to 398.95, 399.69, 401.05 eV [29–31]. In the O1s spectra in Fig. 1(e), OC, O–Si, OC correspond to 530.79, 531.73, 534.99 eV [32]. In Si 2p spectra in Fig. 1(f), Si–C, Si–N, Si–O correspond to 101.60, 102.19, 102.82 eV [31]. Comparison of the XPS full spectra of CMCS in Fig. S1, C1s spectra, N1s spectra and O1s spectra, the appearance of N-Si peaks, O-Si peaks and Si elemental peaks proved the successful introduction of APTES. The results of the XPS spectra were in agreement with those of the FTIR spectra, which proved that the surface of Si-CDs possessed an abundance of reactive groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups.

The TEM image of Si-CDs in Fig. 1(g) shows that they are spherical and without any aggregation and well dispersed in water. The corresponding histogram of size distribution in Fig. 1(i) shows that the average size is 3.15 nm and the particle size is in line with the particle size range of carbon dots [22]. The HRTEM image with higher resolution in Fig. 1(h) shows diffraction streaks of Si-CDs with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [33], which suggests that the core of Si-CDs is mainly carbon. Combined with the results of HRTEM, FTIR and XPS, it can be hypothesized that the Si-CDs is a structure of Si-O-Si skeleton encapsulating a graphitic carbon core.

The FTIR spectra of CMC and DCG are shown in Fig. 2 (a). In the infrared spectra of pure CMC powder, the telescopic vibrational peak of –OH at 3285 cm−1 [34]. The peaks at 2917 cm−1 and 2871 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric telescopic vibrational absorption peaks of the CH bond in –CH2– and –CH3– [35]. The peaks at 1590 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 are attributed to the antisymmetric –COO– and symmetric telescopic vibrational absorption peaks [36]. The telescopic vibrational absorption peak of glycosidic bond on cellulose pyran ring at 1020 cm−1 [37]. In the infrared spectra of DCG, the CH asymmetric stretching vibrational peaks and symmetric stretching vibrational peaks at 2922 cm−1 and 2878 cm−1 were blue shifted, the intensity of the amino peaks near 3400 cm−1 increased, and a new stretching vibrational absorption peak of –CO–NH– appeared at 1640 cm−1 [38]. The formation of chemical bonding connections between Si-CDs and CMCs was suggested, indicating that the amino group of Si-CDs underwent a reaction with the carboxyl group of CMC to form an amide bond, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of DCG.

In order to further investigate the molecular structure and functional group composition of DCG, it was subjected to XPS tests. The full XPS spectra of DCG in Fig. 2(b) shows that DCG contains elements such as C, N, O, and Si. In the C1s spectra in Fig. 2(c), C–Si, CC, CN, CO and CO correspond to 283.97, 284.8, 285.55, 286.9, 288.18 eV, respectively [39], and the appearance of distinct C–Si and CN peaks indicates the successful introduction of Si-CDs. In N1s spectra in Fig. 2(d), CN, NH–CO and NH correspond to 398.8, 399.73, 400.94 eV [40], respectively, and the appearance of NH–CO peaks suggests that the Si-CDs and the CMC are chemically linked by amide bonds. In the O1s spectra in Fig. 2(e), OH, OC, O–Si and OC correspond to 530.81, 531.81, 532.5, 533.11 eV, respectively. in the Si 2p spectra in Fig. 2(f), Si–C, Si–N and Si–O correspond to 100.97, 101.76, 102.3 eV, respectively [31]. Comparing the XPS full spectra, C1s spectra and O1s spectra of CMC in Fig. S2, the appearance of peaks related to N and Si proved the successful reaction of DCG with Si-CDs. The XPS results were in agreement with the FTIR results, which proved the successful synthesis of DCG.

As shown in Fig. 2(g) shows the normalized UV absorption spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG, and DCG/Salt, the shoulder peak at about 340 nm is attributed to the n → π* leap peak of –NH2 [41], and the intensity of the peak of –NH2 decreases after the reaction of Si-CDs with CMC. The peak at about 270 nm is attributed to the n → π* leap peak of CO [42]. Upon adsorption of the salt by DCG, the characteristic n → π* leap peaks of CO of several DCG/Salt were blue shifted to a certain extent, which was brought about by the coordination and electrostatic interaction of metal ions and carboxyl groups in the salt [43]. The action of the extra-nuclear electrons of the halogens produces a red shift in the n → π* lepton peak of CO [44], and thus the n → π* lepton of DCG/ZnSO4 has the largest n → π* lepton blue shift for CO. The microstructures of the prepared gels can be obtained by observing the cross sections using SEM, which is discussed in comparison with DCG/ZnSO4 as an example, and the SEM images of DCG and DCG/ are shown in Fig. 2(h) and Fig. 2(i). DCG shows a porous microstructure, and the adsorption of the metal salt ZnSO4 did not lead to the collapse of the gel structure, and it made the sheet more flat.

XRD spectra of CMC, Si-CDs and DCG are shown in Fig S3, all of which are amorphous structures [45]. Thermogravimetric analysis was utilized to verify the thermal stability of CMC, Si-CDs and DCG as shown in Fig. S4 The weight loss is mainly divided into two phases. The weight loss in the first phase from 50 °C to 230 °C causes the successive evaporation of free and bound water physically adsorbed by the materials. After the beginning of the second phase from 230 °C to 800 °C, the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the materials decompose as the temperature increases [39]. The thermal stability of Si-CDs is high due to the presence of the Si-O-Si skeleton, and the pyrolysis after 230 °C is slow, with more than 50 % of the total weight remaining. The final mass of DCG remaining is 30 %, which is slightly less than that of sodium carboxymethylcellulose due to the lower sodium content in DCG caused by the participation of –COO– in the CMC in the cross-linking reaction.

Metal cation screening was carried out because metal cations may cause quenching of photoluminescent materials. Several DCG/Salt (DCG/MgCl2, DCG/CaCl2, DCG/BaCl2, DCG/AlCl3, DCG/ZnCl2, DCG/MnCl2, DCG/CuCl2, DCG/CoCl2, DCG/NiCl2, DCG/CrCl3 and DCG/FeCl3) obtained by the addition of several chlorinated salts to DCG were observed under the 365 nm UV lamp. DCG/CrCl3 and DCG/FeCl3). The results are shown in Fig. S5 In comparison with DCG, fluorescence emission and phosphorescence emission were still present for DCG/MgCl2, DCG/CaCl2, DCG/BaCl2, DCG/ZnCl2, and DCG/AlCl3, while DCG/MnCl2, DCG/CuCl2, DCG/CoCl2, DCG/NiCl2, DCG/CrCl3, and DCG/FeCl3, DCG/CuCl2, DCG/CoCl2, DCG/NiCl2, DCG/CrCl3, and DCG/FeCl3 were almost completely quenched. divalent metal cations such as Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, and Zn2+ do not quench the fluorescence and phosphorescence of DCG, while most of the trivalent metal cations quench the fluorescence and phosphorescence of DCG. Therefore, in this work, the effects of several divalent metal cations (Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+) and several anions (Cl-, Br-, SO4 2-) on the afterglow duration of the DCGs were mainly explored. The adsorption of DCGs with ZnSO4, ZnCl2, MgCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, and AlCl3 had varying degrees of prolonged phosphorescence lifetime, and the phosphorescence lifetime decreased to a certain extent after adsorption of ZnBr2, which is related to the heavy atom effect of halogens [44].

In order to investigate the effect of the amount of salt on phosphorescence, a series of DCG/Salt were prepared by varying the salt concentration. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), DCG adsorbed with different concentrations of salt exhibited different phosphorescence lifetime durations. As the salt concentration increases, the afterglow duration of DCG/ZnSO4, DCG/ZnCl2, DCG/MgCl2, DCG/CaCl2, and DCG/BaCl2 increases, and stabilizes after the salt concentration reaches 0.4 M. Therefore, it is not specifically stated that the salt concentrations used for the preparation of DCG/Salt in this study are all 0.4 M. As the salt concentration continued to increase, the afterglow duration stabilized with a certain degree of decrease, which is due to the fact that the high concentration of zinc salt is more acidic may have caused damage to the gel. In addition, the excess salt is a non-luminous substance, which reduces the relative amount of phosphorescent substance per unit area. The order of afterglow duration is DCG/ZnBr2 < DCG < DCG/BaCl2 < DCG/CaCl2 < DCG/MgCl2 < DCG/ZnCl2 < DCG/ZnSO4. The afterglow duration of DCG/Salt at 0.4 M salt concentration is shown in Fig. 3(b) (the afterglow duration of Si-CDs could not be recorded by this method because the afterglow duration of Si-CDs is less than 1/30 s). The afterglows of DCG and DCG/Salt range from 3.20 s to 11.86 s with a relatively evenly distribution, and they can be basically differentiated by the naked eye, which makes the dynamic display of the afterglows of the materials easier. This lays the foundation for the dynamic display of material afterglows.

Observed under visible light, Si-CDs is yellow, DCG and DCG/Salt are light yellow. And when observed under UV light, Si-CDs, DCG and DCG/Salt showed blue fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/Salt are shown in Fig. 3(c). The fluorescence emission peaks of DCG are blue shifted in comparison with those of Si-CDs, and the emission centers of DCG and DCG/Salt are about 445 nm at 365 nm excitation light, which is a good match with the corresponding fluorescence excitation spectra. ZnSO4, ZnCl2, MgCl2, CaCl2, and BaCl2 had little effect on the fluorescence emission intensity of DCG. ZnBr2 caused a more pronounced bursting of the fluorescence emission of DCG, but still maintained a high fluorescence intensity, which does not limit its application in the field of anti-counterfeiting. The phosphorescent emission spectra of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG, and DCG/Salt are shown in Fig. 3(d). Si-CDs has only relatively weak phosphorescence emission, and the phosphorescence intensity is significantly increased by reacting with CMC, and then further increased by adsorption of metal salts. Due to the characteristic effects of different metal cations and different anions, DCG/Salt exhibited different phosphorescence emission compared to DCG. The phosphor excitation spectra at the corresponding emission peaks of Si-CDs, CMC, DCG and DCG/ ZnSO4 are shown in Fig. 3(e). The peaks of DCG and DCG/ ZnSO4 at about 350 nm are similar to their fluorescence excitation spectra, which proves that the Si-CDs portion in DCG is the main luminescence center of the gel material.

B i
τ i

The phosphorescence decay curves of Si-CDs, DCG, and DCG/Salt at the peak of excitation with a 365-nm light source at 298 K can be well fitted to equation (1), with the corresponding fitting parameters listed in Table S1. The fitted curves are shown in Fig. 3(f) These materials possess different phosphorescence decay modes. The corresponding phosphorescence lifetimes [46] were calculated using equation (2) and the results are shown in Fig. 3(g). The phosphorescence lifetimes of Si-CDs and CMC were 9.48 ms and 108.58 ms, and the phosphorescence lifetime of DCG obtained after crosslinking the two was significantly enhanced up to 179.55 ms. The phosphorescence lifetimes of DCG/ZnSO4, DCG/ZnCl2, DCG/MgCl2, DCG/CaCl2, and DCG/BaCl2, which were obtained after adsorption of metal salts, all have further significant enhancement, with DCG/ZnSO4 having the longest phosphorescence lifetime of 492.39 ms. To explore the phosphorescence enhancement mechanism, anions and cations are discussed separately. Br and Cl are halogenated elements and SO4 2- is an oxygenate. The phosphorescence lifetime length ordering and phosphorescence emission intensity ordering of DCG/ZnSO4, DCG/ZnCl2 and DCG/ZnBr2 are exactly opposite to each other, which is related to the heavy-atom effect of halogens. DCG/ZnBr2 has stronger phosphorescence emission but shorter phosphorescence lifetime compared to DCG due to the high nucleus electrons of bromine, which is similar to the results of other studies [47]. Zn is a second subgroup element, which is rich in empty orbitals, and the atomic radii of Mg, Ca, and Ba are all second main group elements, and the atomic radius is Mg < Ca < Ba. Due to the similar chemical properties of congeners, the relationship between DCG/ MgCl2, DCG/ CaCl2 and DCG/ BaCl2 is further discussed. The relationship between cation radius and phosphor lifetime and afterglow duration is shown in Fig. 3(h). The smaller the ionic radius, the longer the corresponding DCG/Salt phosphor lifetime and afterglow duration. Therefore, the enhancement of DCG phosphorescence by salt may be related to the coordination and electrostatic effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DCG/Salt structural and enhanced phosphorescence mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the structure and phosphorescence enhancement mechanism of DCG/Salt, we performed XPS tests on them, and the results of the high-resolution XPS spectra were processed by split-peak fitting [48], to compare the binding energies of each chemical bond of DCG and DCG/Salt, as shown in Fig. 4 The peaks of CN, CO, CO, NH, and NH–CO were shifted towards higher binding energies due to the strong interactions of the metal cations with the lone electron pairs on the O and N atoms [49], indicating that hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, and amide groups on the Si-CDs portion of DCG and the CMC portion of DCG are all liganded and electrostatically interacting with the salt.

The structural schematic of DCG/Salt is derived from the structural analysis results, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) the Si-CDs are confined in a rigid mesh structure after cross-linking with the surrounding entangled CMCs via amide bonds, and the coordination and electrostatic interactions between the Si-CDs portion of the salt and the CMC portion of the salt in the DCG further increase the rigidity of the surface groups of the Si-CDs. From this, the mechanism of the enhanced phosphorescence property can be inferred, as shown in Fig. 5(b), Si-CDs absorbs energy and leaps from the ground state S0 to the excited state Sn, which undergoes a relaxation transition to S1. However, its S1 to Tn transition is forbidden, so the Si-CDs can not produce phosphorescence that is visible to the naked eye. the cross-linking of Si-CDs with CMCs produces an oxygen-containing group on the CMCs that is coupled with Si-CDs. a spin–orbit coupling (SOC) effect, which increased the transition from excited singlet to excited triplet states through intersystem crossing (ISC) and activated the phosphorescence emission [16]. Meanwhile, conformational rigidity induced by the restrictive effect of cellulose chains and chemical bonding connections limits the nonradiative leaps due to the motion of the surface groups of Si-CDs, which reduces the phosphorescence attenuation and increases the phosphorescence lifetime [50]. The introduction of metal salts further contributes to the activation of phosphorescence and reduction of attenuation, while different metal salts bring about specific effects that give DCG/Salt different phosphorescent properties.

Different kinds of non-precious metal salts can be used to modulate the afterglow duration of DCG within a certain range, so that it will be extinguished sequentially after the UV lamp is turned off, thus displaying dynamic patterns (The full video is shown in Video S1 and Video S2). The DCG/Salt (DCG/ ZnBr2, DCG/ BaCl2, DCG/ BaCl2, DCG/ CaCl2, DCG/ MgCl2, DCG/ ZnSO4) and Si-CDs with different afterglow durations obtained by salt modulation were used to form two dynamic multilevel anticounterfeiting patterns of digits and flowers, and the materials and their positions in the patterns are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The digital security pattern shown in Fig. 6(b) is white to yellowish in daylight, as shown in Fig. 6(c) Under the excitation of 365 nm UV lamp, it shows a blue fluorescent pattern “8″. After the UV lamp is turned off, it shows a series of blue-green phosphorescent patterns, which are extinguished sequentially with different afterglow lengths of the material, as shown in Fig. 6(d) The patterns ”9″, “3″, ”7 “, ”1″. The number 9 is displayed at 0.5 s, the number 3 at 1.5 s, the number 7 at 4.5 s, and the number 1 at 6 s. In addition, the pattern made with DCG/Salt simulated a wilting flower as shown in Fig. 6(e). When the UV lamp was turned off, the DCG/Salt with different afterglow durations on the stamen and each petal went out in sequence, just like a flower withering gradually. These results demonstrate its potential application as a luminescent anti-counterfeiting icon material.

In conclusion, we have developed an effective universal gel platform for cellulose-based tunable afterglow duration materials, which absorbs non-precious metal salts through the adsorption of the gel itself, and enhances its phosphorescence properties through intersystem crossing (ISC) and conformational restriction. The afterglow duration can be regulated by the characteristic effects of different metal salts, which is a simple phosphorescence modulation method. The phosphorescence lifetime of the gel can reach 492.39 ms after absorbing ZnSO4, which significantly improves the phosphorescence lifetime and afterglow duration of the carbon dots. By regulating the afterglow duration of the material within a certain range, the material realizes multistage anticounterfeiting modes under daylight, under UV light excitation, and after the light is turned off, and more importantly, it realizes the dynamic information display of the afterglow, which creates more possibilities for future anti-counterfeiting.

Zhengdong Zhao: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuan Shen: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yang Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Jiaqi Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Mingjian Ma: Methodology, Investigation. Jiangbo Pan: Methodology, Investigation. Di Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Chengyu Wang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jian Li: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (GN: 31770593) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (GN: 2572023AW50). The authors also would like to acknowledge the technical support from Analysis and Testing Center of Northeast Forestry University.
